source.title: 2017 ACC/AHA/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/AGS/APhA/ASH/ASPC/NMA/PCNA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
source.organization: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

signals: Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure
management of high blood pressure in adults
American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association

extraction.summary: The 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults provides comprehensive recommendations for hypertension management. It emphasizes accurate BP measurement, lifestyle modifications, and pharmacological therapy based on cardiovascular risk assessment. The guideline defines new BP categories and treatment thresholds, with emphasis on achieving optimal BP control to reduce cardiovascular events.

population.age_criteria: Adults (18+ years), with special considerations for older persons and children/adolescents
population.risk_criteria: Based on overall cardiovascular disease risk assessment and comorbidities
population.disease_definition: High blood pressure defined by BP thresholds and categories as per guideline classification
population.exclusions: Pregnancy-specific considerations, acute medical conditions requiring specialized management
population.severity_staging: BP categories: Normal, Elevated, Stage 1 Hypertension, Stage 2 Hypertension

key_thresholds.context: Primary method with ambulatory BP monitoring for confirmation
Office SBP/DBP higher than home or ABPM readings
Agreement for white coat hypertension detection
Minimum level to interpret aldosterone:renin ratio as positive

key_thresholds.parameter: SBP treatment threshold (primary prevention high risk)
DBP treatment threshold (primary prevention high risk)
SBP treatment threshold (primary prevention low risk)
DBP treatment threshold (primary prevention low risk)

key_thresholds.context: Adults with confirmed hypertension and known CVD or high CVD risk
Adults with confirmed hypertension and known CVD or high CVD risk
Less than threshold for patients with stable ischemic heart disease

key_thresholds.parameter: Blood pressure treatment threshold in SIHD
key_thresholds.context: Initiate medications at or above this level
Less than threshold for patients with chronic kidney disease

key_thresholds.parameter: Albuminuria threshold for ACE inhibitor consideration
key_thresholds.context: CKD patients with albuminuria at this level or higher
Acceptable increase with ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy in CKD
Continuous IV infusion recommended above this level

key_thresholds.parameter: BP threshold acute ischemic stroke thrombolysis
key_thresholds.context: Must be below before tissue plasminogen activator
Treatment reduces progression to severe hypertension by 50%

recommendations.recommendation: Use accurate BP measurement techniques in office settings
recommendations.notes: Proper technique essential for diagnosis and management

recommendations.recommendation: Consider ambulatory BP monitoring for suspected white coat or masked hypertension
recommendations.notes: Superior to office measurements for certain populations

recommendations.recommendation: Implement lifestyle modifications as first-line therapy
recommendations.notes: Weight reduction, dietary changes, physical activity, alcohol limitation

recommendations.recommendation: Initiate pharmacological therapy based on BP thresholds and CV risk
recommendations.notes: Treatment decisions should incorporate overall cardiovascular risk

recommendations.recommendation: BP should be categorized as normal, elevated, or stage 1 or 2 hypertension to prevent and treat high BP
recommendations.notes: Based on observational data and clinical trial evidence

recommendations.recommendation: Screening for and management of other modifiable CVD risk factors are recommended in adults with hypertension
recommendations.notes: Multiple CVD risk factors common in hypertensive patients

recommendations.recommendation: Use proper methods for accurate measurement and documentation of BP for diagnosis and management of high BP
recommendations.notes: Follow checklist including 5-minute rest period, proper positioning, correct cuff size

recommendations.recommendation: Out-of-office BP measurements recommended to confirm diagnosis of hypertension and for medication titration with telehealth counseling
recommendations.notes: Systematic review evidence supports out-of-office monitoring

recommendations.recommendation: Screen for white coat hypertension using daytime ABPM or HBPM in adults with untreated SBP 130-160 mmHg or DBP 80-100 mmHg
recommendations.notes: Reasonable to screen before diagnosis

recommendations.recommendation: Screen for masked hypertension with HBPM in adults with untreated office BP 120-129/75-79 mmHg
recommendations.notes: Reasonable screening approach

recommendations.recommendation: Screening for specific forms of secondary hypertension when clinical indications are present or in adults with resistant hypertension
recommendations.notes: Based on clinical findings and physical examination

recommendations.recommendation: Referral to physician with expertise for diagnostic confirmation and treatment of secondary hypertension
recommendations.notes: When screening positive for secondary hypertension

recommendations.recommendation: Screen for primary aldosteronism in adults with hypertension and concurrent resistant hypertension, hypokalemia, incidental adrenal mass, family history of early-onset hypertension, or stroke at young age
recommendations.notes: Primary aldosteronism occurs in 5-10% of hypertensive patients

recommendations.recommendation: Use plasma aldosterone:renin activity ratio for screening primary aldosteronism
recommendations.notes: Most accurate and reliable screening method

recommendations.recommendation: Refer adults with positive primary aldosteronism screening to hypertension specialist or endocrinologist
recommendations.notes: For further evaluation and treatment

recommendations.recommendation: Medical therapy is recommended for adults with atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis
recommendations.notes: No RCT has shown benefit of revascularization over medical therapy

recommendations.recommendation: Weight loss is recommended to reduce BP in adults with elevated BP or hypertension who are overweight or obese
recommendations.notes: Expect about 1 mmHg reduction per 1-kg weight loss

recommendations.recommendation: DASH diet is recommended for adults with elevated BP or hypertension
recommendations.notes: Heart-healthy diet that facilitates achieving desirable weight

recommendations.recommendation: Sodium reduction is recommended for adults with elevated BP or hypertension
recommendations.notes: Optimal goal <1500 mg/day, aim for at least 1000 mg/day reduction

recommendations.recommendation: Potassium supplementation is recommended for adults with elevated BP or hypertension
recommendations.notes: Unless contraindicated by CKD or drugs that reduce potassium excretion

recommendations.recommendation: Increased physical activity with structured exercise program is recommended
recommendations.notes: For adults with elevated BP or hypertension

recommendations.recommendation: Adults with elevated BP or hypertension who consume alcohol should limit intake to ≤2 drinks/day (men) and ≤1 drink/day (women)
recommendations.notes: Standard drink contains ~14g pure alcohol

recommendations.recommendation: Use BP-lowering medications for secondary prevention in patients with clinical CVD and average SBP ≥130 mmHg or DBP ≥80 mmHg, and for primary prevention in adults with 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10% and average SBP ≥130 mmHg or DBP ≥80 mmHg
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Use BP-lowering medication for primary prevention in adults with no CVD history, 10-year ASCVD risk <10%, and SBP ≥140 mmHg or DBP ≥90 mmHg
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Implement nonpharmacological interventions including weight loss, DASH diet, sodium reduction, potassium enhancement, physical activity, and moderate alcohol consumption
recommendations.notes: Core interventions for all hypertensive patients

recommendations.recommendation: Use BP-lowering medications for secondary prevention with SBP ≥130 mmHg or DBP ≥80 mmHg, and for primary prevention with 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10% and BP ≥130/80 mmHg
recommendations.evidence_level: A for SBP, C-EO for DBP
recommendations.notes: Based on meta-analyses and RCTs

recommendations.recommendation: Use BP-lowering medications for primary prevention with ASCVD risk <10% and BP ≥140/90 mmHg
recommendations.notes: Cost-effective treatment threshold

recommendations.recommendation: Stage 1 hypertension with low ASCVD risk should be managed with nonpharmacological therapy with repeat evaluation in 3-6 months
recommendations.notes: Lifestyle modifications first

recommendations.recommendation: Stage 2 hypertension should be treated with combination therapy and evaluated within 1 month
recommendations.notes: Two agents of different classes

recommendations.recommendation: Very high BP (≥180/110 mmHg) requires prompt evaluation and treatment
recommendations.notes: Reduce risk of target organ damage

recommendations.recommendation: Do not use ACE inhibitor, ARB, and/or renin inhibitor simultaneously
recommendations.notes: Increases cardiovascular and renal risk

recommendations.recommendation: For adults with confirmed hypertension and known CVD or 10-year ASCVD event risk ≥10%, a BP target of <130/80 mmHg
recommendations.notes: SBP evidence B-RSR, DBP evidence C-EO

recommendations.recommendation: First-line agents include thiazide diuretics, CCBs, and ACE inhibitors or ARBs
recommendations.notes: Systematic review evidence

recommendations.recommendation: Initiate therapy with 2 first-line agents in stage 2 hypertension
recommendations.notes: Expert opinion based on average BP >20/10 mmHg above target

recommendations.recommendation: Monthly follow-up until BP control achieved
recommendations.notes: Randomized trial evidence

recommendations.recommendation: In adults with SIHD and hypertension, a BP target of less than 130/80 mmHg is recommended
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Adults with SIHD and hypertension should be treated with medications (e.g., beta blockers, ACE inhibitors, or ARBs) for compelling indications as first-line therapy
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation with addition of other drugs as needed

recommendations.recommendation: Adults with hypertension and CKD should be treated to a BP goal of less than 130/80 mmHg
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: In adults with hypertension and CKD (stage 3 or higher or stage 1-2 with albuminuria), treatment with an ACE inhibitor is reasonable to slow kidney disease progression
recommendations.notes: Class IIa recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Nondihydropyridine CCBs are not recommended in the treatment of hypertension in adults with HFrEF
recommendations.notes: Class III: No Benefit recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: After kidney transplantation, treat patients with hypertension to a BP goal of less than 130/80 mm Hg
recommendations.notes: Class IIa recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: In adults with diabetes and hypertension, initiate antihypertensive drug treatment at BP of 130/80 mm Hg or higher with treatment goal of less than 130/80 mm Hg
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Adults with acute ischemic stroke and elevated BP who are eligible for IV tissue plasminogen activator should have BP slowly lowered to less than 185/110 mm Hg before thrombolytic therapy
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Immediate lowering of SBP to less than 140 mm Hg in adults with spontaneous ICH within 6 hours is not of benefit and can be potentially harmful
recommendations.notes: Class III: Harm recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: ASCVD risk assessment in all adults with hypertension, including adults with diabetes
recommendations.notes: Can assume majority of adults with DM have 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%

recommendations.recommendation: Treatment of hypertension with an ARB can be useful for prevention of recurrence of atrial fibrillation
recommendations.notes: Class IIa recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: In adults with asymptomatic aortic stenosis, hypertension should be treated with pharmacotherapy, starting at a low dose and gradually titrating upward as needed
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Beta blockers are recommended as the preferred antihypertensive agents in patients with hypertension and thoracic aortic disease
recommendations.notes: Based on expert opinion

recommendations.recommendation: In black adults with hypertension but without HF or CKD, including those with DM, initial antihypertensive treatment should include a thiazide-type diuretic or CCB
recommendations.notes: Class I recommendation

recommendations.recommendation: Treatment of hypertension with SBP goal <130 mmHg for noninstitutionalized community-dwelling adults ≥65 years
recommendations.notes: Based on RCTs including SPRINT and HYVET

recommendations.recommendation: Transition pregnant women to methyldopa, nifedipine, and/or labetalol
recommendations.notes: For women with hypertension who become pregnant

recommendations.recommendation: Do not use ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or direct renin inhibitors in pregnancy
recommendations.notes: Class III Harm - fetotoxic effects

recommendations.recommendation: ICU admission for hypertensive emergencies with continuous BP monitoring
recommendations.notes: For parenteral administration of appropriate agents

medications.indication: LDL cholesterol reduction in renal artery stenosis
Long-acting diuretic, effective for LVH reduction
Superior to beta blockers for LVH reduction

medications.drug: Beta blockers (carvedilol, metoprolol tartrate, metoprolol succinate, nadolol, bisoprolol, propranolol, timolol)

medications.indication: SIHD with persistent angina and uncontrolled hypertension
HFpEF with volume overload, additional BP control

medications.dose: Initial 5mg/h, increase by 2.5mg/h every 5 min to max 15mg/h
Loading 500-1000 mcg/kg/min over 1 min, then 50 mcg/kg/min infusion

red_flags: Hypertensive crisis with systolic BP >180 or diastolic BP >120 mmHg
Signs of target organ damage
Acute cardiovascular events
Suspected secondary hypertension
Resistant hypertension despite multiple medications
Pregnancy with elevated BP
White coat effect with office SBP/DBP >20/10 mmHg higher than home/ABPM readings
Masked hypertension with normal office BP but elevated out-of-office readings
Target organ damage in presence of controlled office BP readings
Rapid cuff deflation during auscultatory readings
Reliance on single occasion BP measurements for diagnosis
Resistant hypertension
Abrupt onset or worsening hypertension
Flash pulmonary edema
Paroxysmal hypertension with spells, headache, sweating, palpitations
Hypertension with hypokalemia
Target organ damage disproportionate to duration/severity
Early-onset hypertension especially in young patients
Resistant hypertension with hypokalemia
Early-onset hypertension with family history
Stroke at young age (<40 years)
Incidentally discovered adrenal mass
Refractory hypertension with worsening renal function
Intractable heart failure in setting of renal artery stenosis
BP lability, episodic pallor and dizziness (pheochromocytoma)
Snoring, hypersomnolence (obstructive sleep apnea)
Prostatism (chronic kidney disease)
Muscle cramps, weakness (hypokalemia)
Weight loss, palpitations, heat intolerance (hyperthyroidism)
Edema, fatigue, frequent urination (kidney disease)
Central obesity, facial rounding, easy bruisability (Cushing's syndrome)
Orthostatic hypotension (decline >20 mmHg SBP or >10 mmHg DBP)
SBP ≥180 mmHg or DBP ≥110 mmHg requiring prompt treatment
New or worsening target organ damage
Signs of hypertensive crisis
Acute renal failure in bilateral renal artery stenosis
Angioedema with ACE inhibitors or ARBs
Abrupt cessation of beta blockers may cause rebound hypertension
Orthostatic hypotension especially with alpha-1 blockers in older adults
Drug-induced lupus-like syndrome with hydralazine at higher doses
Hypokalemia requiring exclusion of primary aldosteronism
Pericardial effusion with minoxidil
Serum creatinine increase >30% with ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy
Hyperkalemia with ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy
Hypotension with intensive BP lowering
Volume contraction with diuretics
Use of nephrotoxic agents in CKD patients
Suspected renovascular disease
SBP greater than 220 mm Hg in acute ICH
Neurological worsening in acute stroke
Hematoma expansion in ICH
Renal adverse events with intensive BP lowering
Anemia, hyperkalemia, and lower GFR with ACE inhibitors post-transplant
Symptomatic ICH in thrombolysis patients
ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema (higher risk in blacks)
Severe hypotension with intensive BP lowering
Reduction in estimated GFR with intensive therapy
Hypokalemia and hyponatremia with diuretics
Fetal harm with ACE inhibitors/ARBs in pregnancy
Aortic dissection or rupture in thoracic aortic disease
SBP >180/120 mmHg with target organ damage
Hypertensive encephalopathy
Acute ICH or ischemic stroke with severe hypertension
Acute MI with hypertensive emergency
Acute LV failure with pulmonary edema
Aortic dissection
Preeclampsia/eclampsia
Pheochromocytoma crisis
1-year death rate >79% if hypertensive emergency untreated
extraction.follow_up: Follow-up after initiation of antihypertensive therapy varies by clinical situation, typically 2-4 weeks initially, then every 1-3 months until BP goal achieved, then every 3-6 months for stable patients
extraction.escalation_path: Escalate to specialist care for resistant hypertension, suspected secondary causes, hypertensive emergencies, or when BP targets cannot be achieved despite optimal therapy. Consider cardiology, nephrology, or endocrinology consultation as appropriate.
